signal: On-page schema vs agentic commerce layer
finding: Product pages carry complete Product JSON-LD (verified: 24 variant offers with price/SKU/availability/brand on the Gravity 1.0 helmet), but the homepage and collection pages carry zero JSON-LD (no Organization, WebSite or CollectionPage/BreadcrumbList). Meanwhile the store exposes a full UCP/MCP agentic-commerce API (search_catalog, create_cart, create_checkout). The two machine-readable layers do not reinforce each other.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Generic crawlers score schema presence per-page-type in isolation; they don't cross-reference a site's structured data against its separate agent/API layer to spot that one channel is rich and the other is empty.

finding: Sampled product (Helmet MTB Gravity 1.0) has 24 variants: 19 OutOfStock, 5 InStock (79% of SKUs unavailable). There is no product-level availability rollup in the schema, only per-variant. Merchant Center / rich-result logic that reads product-level signals can under-represent an item that still has live stock in some sizes/colors.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Tools that validate schema check for required fields' presence/validity, not the stock-mix ratio behind them or how that ratio could affect the whole product's shopping eligibility.

finding: Verified directly: fetching a size/color variant URL (?variant=...) returns a canonical tag and og:url pointing back to the base product URL, not the variant URL. This is the correct behavior and was confirmed by fetching a live variant URL and diffing its <head> against the base product page — not assumed from platform defaults.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Most tools flag 'Shopify variant URLs may cause duplicate content' as a generic warning without ever fetching a live variant URL to check whether canonicalization is actually implemented correctly.

finding: All 1,199 URLs sampled from sitemap_products_1.xml carry the identical <lastmod> value, matching the exact moment the sitemap was requested (not each product's real last-edit time). This makes lastmod useless as a freshness/priority signal — Google has stated it distrusts sitemaps where lastmod doesn't vary meaningfully across URLs.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Tools check 'does the sitemap have lastmod: yes/no' as a binary pass, they don't compare lastmod values ACROSS entries to check whether the field is real per-URL data or a generation-time stamp copied onto every row.

finding: Response header reads Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=0; includeSubDomains; preload — the max-age=0 value tells browsers to stop enforcing HTTPS-only immediately, which contradicts the includeSubDomains/preload flags sitting right next to it. Confirmed via direct curl -I against the live homepage.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Most SEO audit tools check only for the presence of a Strict-Transport-Security header (pass/fail), not its max-age value — a header can be 'present' and still be a functional no-op.

finding: https://leatt.com/ (no za subdomain) returns a 301 to https://za.leatt.com/. Given the ZA localization cookie and country=ZA server-timing data seen on za.leatt.com, this is most likely Shopify Markets IP-based geo-redirection rather than a single hard-coded redirect for all visitors — but this was only verified from one vantage point (this session's egress IP) and should be re-checked from a non-ZA IP before treating it as confirmed behavior for all regions.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Automated crawlers run from a single data-center IP and would report this as one static 301, without knowing (or checking) that Shopify Markets can serve a different result by visitor geography.

signal: Disallow rule for a dead URL
finding: robots.txt explicitly disallows /76859474144 — a bare numeric path with no readable context. Fetching it directly returns a live 404. The rule is harmless (blocking an already-dead page) but indicates leftover cruft from a removed product/page that was never cleaned out of the rules file.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Generic robots.txt validators check syntax validity, not whether each individual Disallow target still resolves to a real, currently-existing resource.

signal: llms.txt / agents.md / UCP present and unblocked
finding: Confirmed by direct fetch: /llms.txt (4.3KB) and /agents.md (4.3KB) both exist, describing a full Universal Commerce Protocol implementation (/.well-known/ucp discovery + POST /api/ucp/mcp with tools/list). robots.txt has no AI-crawler-specific disallow (no GPTBot/ClaudeBot/PerplexityBot/CCBot blocks) — nothing here is fencing the catalog off from AI answer engines or shopping agents.
why_regular_seo_tools_miss_it: Almost no mainstream SEO tool checks for llms.txt, agents.md, or UCP/MCP endpoints yet — this is 2026-era agentic-commerce infrastructure that predates most audit-tool checklists.
